playbooks.description: Multiple failed authentication attempts followed by a successful login or a sustained high volume of failed attempts targeting an account or service.
indicators: High volume of Event ID 4625 (Failed Logon)
Login attempts from multiple geolocations in a short timeframe
Targeting of default administrative accounts (e.g., admin, root, administrator)
detection_patterns: Count of failed logins > 20 within 5 minutes for a single user
Failed logins from > 5 distinct IPs for a single user within 15 minutes
Failed login burst followed by successful login (Event ID 4624) from the same IP
response_steps: Verify the source IP of the anomalous login attempts.
Determine if a successful login occurred after the failed attempts.
Check for subsequent anomalous activity (e.g., MFA modification, new inbox rules) if login was successful.
Identify if the targeted service is internal or externally facing.
investigation_queries: index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security EventCode=4625 | stats count by TargetUserName, IpAddress | where count > 20
SigninLogs | where ResultType == '50126' | summarize count() by UserPrincipalName, IPAddress | where count_ > 50
false_positive_checks: Verify if the user recently changed their password and has stale cached credentials on a mobile device or background service.
Check if the source IP is a known corporate gateway or NAT IP causing aggregated login counts.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: At {{time}}, SOC detected a brute force attack originating from {{source_ip}} targeting user {{username}}. {{failed_count}} failed attempts were observed followed by {{successful_or_not}} login. Passwords were reset and IP blocked. No post-compromise activity observed.

playbooks.description: An email containing a malicious link designed to steal user credentials by spoofing a legitimate corporate or third-party login page.
indicators: Suspicious sender domains resembling legitimate services (e.g., mircosoft-support.com)
Use of URL shorteners or redirectors in email body
Subject lines creating false urgency (e.g., 'Action Required: Password Expiry')
detection_patterns: Email rule matching known phishing keywords + external sender
User click detected on URL categorized as 'Newly Registered Domain' or 'Phishing' by web proxy
response_steps: Analyze the email headers to identify the true sender and delivery path.
Extract and detonate the URL in a sandbox to determine the payload/behavior.
Search mail flow logs to identify all recipients of the campaign.
Check web proxy/DNS logs to identify which users successfully clicked and reached the malicious domain.
investigation_queries: index=email sourcetype=proofpoint recipient=* subject='*Password Expiry*' | table sender, recipient, url
DeviceNetworkEvents | where RemoteUrl contains 'malicious-domain.com' | project DeviceName, InitiatingProcessAccountName, Timestamp
false_positive_checks: Verify if the email is an authorized internal phishing simulation.
Check if the sender is a known marketing or legitimate third-party vendor misidentified by heuristics.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: A phishing campaign utilizing the subject '{{subject}}' was delivered to {{recipient_count}} users. Analysis confirmed the payload was a credential harvesting page. {{click_count}} users clicked the link. Impacted user accounts were disabled, passwords reset, and the malicious domain {{domain}} was blocked at the proxy.

playbooks.description: Detection of tools, behaviors, or file modifications indicative of ransomware deployment, such as shadow copy deletion or mass file encryption.
indicators: Execution of 'vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet'
Mass file renaming with unusual extensions (e.g., .encrypted, .locky)
Creation of 'read_me.txt' or 'decrypt_instructions.html' in multiple directories
detection_patterns: Endpoint EDR alert for shadow copy deletion
High volume of file modifications/creations in a short time span (>1000 files/min) by a single process
Detection of known ransomware precursor tools (Cobalt Strike, PsExec, Rclone)
response_steps: IMMEDIATELY isolate the affected host(s) from the network.
Identify the parent process executing the encryption or shadow copy deletion.
Identify the compromised account used to execute the payload.
Hunt for lateral movement indicators to identify patient zero and other compromised hosts.
Preserve memory and disk artifacts for forensics before rebooting or reimaging.
investigation_queries: index=edr sourcetype=crowdstrike ProcessName=vssadmin.exe CommandLine='*delete shadows*'
DeviceProcessEvents | where FileName in~ ('vssadmin.exe', 'wbadmin.exe', 'bcdedit.exe') and ProcessCommandLine has_any ('delete', 'recoveryenabled no')
false_positive_checks: Confirm if the activity is related to an approved IT backup software installation or server migration script.
Verify if the mass file modification is due to a legitimate developer script compiling large code bases.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: CRITICAL INCIDENT: Ransomware behavior detected on {{host_count}} hosts. Initial vector identified as {{initial_vector}}. Containment achieved via network isolation. Active directory accounts {{compromised_accounts}} have been disabled. Incident Response retainer activated.

playbooks.description: Execution of PowerShell commands containing encoded payloads, hidden windows, or execution policy bypasses commonly used by malware.
indicators: Use of -EncodedCommand, -enc, or -e parameters
Use of -WindowStyle Hidden or -w hidden
Use of -ExecutionPolicy Bypass or -ep bypass
Presence of WebClient.DownloadString or Invoke-WebRequest in command line
detection_patterns: PowerShell executed with a base64 string longer than 200 characters
PowerShell spawning from an unusual parent process (e.g., winword.exe, excel.exe, w3wp.exe)
response_steps: Extract and decode the base64 payload to determine the script's intent.
Check if the script initiated any network connections.
Identify if the script dropped any secondary payloads or established persistence (e.g., scheduled tasks, registry run keys).
Review the parent process to determine how PowerShell was invoked.
false_positive_checks: Verify if the script is part of an SCCM/Intune deployment.
Check if legitimate IT administration tools (e.g., RMM agents) utilize encoded PowerShell for routine tasks.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: Suspicious PowerShell utilizing {{obfuscation_technique}} was detected on {{hostname}}. Decoding the payload revealed an attempt to {{script_action}}. The endpoint was isolated and the command and control IP {{c2_ip}} was blocked on the firewall.

playbooks.description: Unauthorized transfer of data from a computer or network, often detected by unusual outbound traffic volumes or use of unapproved cloud storage.
indicators: Large outbound network spikes (>1GB) to unknown external IPs or file-sharing sites (Mega, Dropbox, Google Drive)
Use of command-line tools for transfer (curl, wget, rclone, ftp) on non-developer endpoints
Creation of large archive files (ZIP, RAR, 7z) in staging directories (e.g., C:Temp, C:UsersPublic)
detection_patterns: Outbound traffic threshold exceeded for a specific user/host baseline
DLP (Data Loss Prevention) alert triggering for PII/PCI data match during upload
response_steps: Identify the user account and host responsible for the data transfer.
Determine the destination IP/URL and what service it belongs to.
Identify the staging directory and analyze the files being compressed/transferred.
Review host timelines to see what processes accessed sensitive files prior to the exfiltration.
investigation_queries: index=firewall action=allowed direction=outbound | stats sum(bytes_out) as total_out by src_ip, dest_ip | where total_out > 1073741824
DeviceNetworkEvents | where ActionType == 'ConnectionSuccess' and RemoteUrl has_any ('mega.nz', 'dropbox.com') | summarize sum(LocalPort) by DeviceName
false_positive_checks: Confirm if the user is performing a legitimate business task, such as uploading marketing videos or sending large datasets to an approved vendor.
Verify if the destination is an approved corporate cloud application.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: Anomalous outbound data transfer of {{data_size}} was detected from host {{hostname}} to external destination {{destination_service}}. Investigation confirmed unauthorized exfiltration of {{data_type}} data by user {{username}}. Network access was blocked and the user's account suspended.

playbooks.description: Automated attempts to compromise user accounts using large lists of leaked usernames and passwords against web applications.
indicators: High volume of failed logins against a web portal originating from datacenter/proxy/Tor IPs
Low ratio of successful logins to failed logins across the application
Login attempts rotating through hundreds of different usernames from a single or small cluster of IPs
detection_patterns: WAF alert for credential stuffing signatures
Distinct IP generating > 100 login requests to /api/v1/login within 5 minutes, targeting different accounts
response_steps: Identify the scope of the attack (target URLs, source IPs, timeframe).
Determine if any accounts were successfully compromised during the attack window.
For compromised accounts, check for fraudulent activity (e.g., unauthorized purchases, data downloads).
Deploy WAF rules to rate-limit or block the attacking infrastructure.
investigation_queries: index=waf sourcetype=f5:bigip:ltm uri='/login' | stats count by client_ip, status | where status=401 and count > 100
AppTraces | where Name == 'POST /login' | summarize count() by ClientIP, ResultCode
false_positive_checks: Verify if the traffic originates from a known vulnerability scanner (e.g., Qualys, Nessus) performing authenticated scans.
Check if an API change caused a legitimate integration to fail authentication repeatedly.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: A credential stuffing attack targeted the {{application_name}} application from {{time_start}} to {{time_end}}. {{failed_count}} failed attempts were logged. {{success_count}} accounts were compromised. Compromised accounts had passwords reset and MFA enforced. Attacking IPs were added to the WAF blocklist.

playbooks.description: Unauthorized access, theft, or sabotage of data/systems by an employee or contractor with legitimate access.
indicators: Accessing sensitive files or databases outside of normal working hours
A departing employee downloading large volumes of CRM data or source code to a USB drive
Privilege escalation attempts or probing of internal systems by a non-IT user
detection_patterns: UBA (User Behavior Analytics) alert for anomalous access to restricted file shares
USB insertion event followed by massive file read operations by a user on the HR termination list
response_steps: Coordinate immediately with HR and Legal before taking aggressive containment actions.
Monitor the user's activity silently to gather evidence if instructed by Legal.
Review endpoint logs for USB usage, cloud storage uploads, and printing activity.
Identify the classification of the data accessed or exfiltrated.
investigation_queries: index=dlp user=* action=USB_Copy | stats count, values(filename) by user
DeviceEvents | where ActionType == 'UsbDriveMounted' | join kind=inner (DeviceFileEvents | where ActionType == 'FileCopied') on DeviceId
false_positive_checks: Verify with the user's manager if the activity was a directed project or legitimate offline work.
Check if the bulk data movement was due to an approved device migration or backup.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: Insider threat activity was detected involving user {{username}}. The user transferred {{data_description}} to an unauthorized USB device. Upon coordination with HR and Legal, the user's access was revoked and the endpoint secured for forensic imaging.

playbooks.description: Detection of known malware families (e.g., Emotet, Trickbot, AgentTesla) executing on an endpoint.
indicators: Antivirus/EDR alerts for known malware signatures or behavioral heuristics
Unexpected execution of processes from AppData\Local\Temp or Roaming directories
Beaconing traffic to known malicious domains or IPs
detection_patterns: EDR block or quarantine event for a Trojan or InfoStealer
Office application (Word, Excel) spawning cmd.exe or powershell.exe
response_steps: Review the EDR alert to determine if the malware was successfully blocked or if execution occurred.
Identify the infection vector (e.g., email attachment, web download, USB).
Check proxy logs to see if the malware successfully communicated with its C2 server.
Perform a full forensic scan of the endpoint to ensure no secondary payloads were dropped.
investigation_queries: index=edr sourcetype=crowdstrike event_simpleName=ProcessRollup2 FileName IN ("*exe", "*dll") FilePath="*\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\*"
DeviceProcessEvents | where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ ('winword.exe', 'excel.exe') and FileName in~ ('cmd.exe', 'powershell.exe')
false_positive_checks: Verify if the alert triggered on a poorly written custom internal application or a legitimate portable app.
Check if the file is a known false positive by searching the hash on VirusTotal.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: Malware identified as {{malware_family}} was detected on {{hostname}}. The payload was delivered via {{delivery_method}}. EDR successfully blocked the execution. The host was temporarily isolated for a full scan, and the user's password was reset out of an abundance of caution.

playbooks.description: An attacker or malicious software exploiting vulnerabilities or misconfigurations to gain SYSTEM or Administrator rights on a host.
indicators: A standard user process unexpectedly spawning a process running as NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM
Modification of system services or creation of new services by non-admin users
Exploitation of known vulnerabilities (e.g., PrintNightmare, ZeroLogon)
detection_patterns: EDR alert for token manipulation or suspicious access to lsass.exe
Creation of a scheduled task running as SYSTEM from a non-privileged context
response_steps: Isolate the host immediately, as the attacker now has full control of the OS.
Identify the exploit or technique used (e.g., unpatched CVE, vulnerable driver, service hijacking).
Review what actions the attacker took after gaining SYSTEM (e.g., credential dumping, lateral movement).
Determine how the initial standard user access was obtained.
investigation_queries: index=windows EventCode=4672 PrivilegeList='*SeDebugPrivilege*' | where NOT user IN ('SYSTEM', 'Administrator')
DeviceProcessEvents | where ProcessTokenElevationType == 'TokenElevationTypeFull' and InitiatingProcessAccountName != 'SYSTEM'
false_positive_checks: Confirm if an IT administrator used Run As Administrator or a PAM solution (e.g., CyberArk, LAPS) to elevate temporarily.
Check if a legitimate software installer or updater requires SYSTEM level execution and triggered heuristics.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: Privilege escalation detected on {{hostname}}. A standard account {{username}} utilized {{exploit_technique}} to gain SYSTEM privileges. Endpoint was isolated. Post-escalation activity included {{post_escalation_activity}}. Host queued for rebuild.

playbooks.description: Techniques used by adversaries to access and control remote systems on a network, expanding their foothold.
indicators: Use of PsExec, WMI, or WinRM originating from standard workstations rather than IT admin jump boxes
Access to administrative shares (C$, ADMIN$) over SMB (Port 445)
Pass-the-Hash (PtH) or Overpass-the-Hash authentication anomalies
detection_patterns: Event ID 4624 Type 3 (Network Logon) to a server using a non-admin account accessing ADMIN$
Service creation (Event ID 7045) containing highly randomized names or known malicious binaries (e.g., PSEXESVC.exe)
response_steps: Identify the source host initiating the lateral movement.
Identify the targeted hosts and verify if the connection was successful.
Determine the compromised credentials being used for the movement.
Look for subsequent execution artifacts (e.g., dropped files, PowerShell commands) on the target host.
investigation_queries: index=windows EventCode=4624 LogonType=3 LogonProcessName=NtLmSsp | stats count by SourceNetworkAddress, TargetUserName, Computer
DeviceEvents | where ActionType == 'ServiceInstalled' and FileName =~ 'PSEXESVC.exe'
false_positive_checks: Verify if the source IP belongs to a vulnerability scanner or an IT management tool (e.g., SCCM, Lansweeper).
Check if a system administrator is performing remote troubleshooting.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: Lateral movement detected originating from {{source_host}} targeting {{target_hosts}}. The adversary utilized {{technique}} (e.g., PsExec, WMI) under the context of compromised account {{username}}. Source and target hosts have been isolated, and credentials rotated.

playbooks.description: Consistent, periodic network traffic from an internal host to an external infrastructure, indicating an established malware C2 channel.
indicators: Regular HTTP/HTTPS requests to an external IP or domain with consistent timing (e.g., exactly every 5 minutes)
Use of non-standard ports or protocols (e.g., IRC, custom TCP protocols)
Domain Generation Algorithm (DGA) domains in DNS queries
High volume of connections to newly registered or uncategorized domains
detection_patterns: Proxy logs showing repetitive requests to the same URL with equal byte sizes
Firewall/Zeek detecting beacon-like timing patterns (low jitter)
EDR alerting on a suspicious process initiating external network connections
response_steps: Isolate the internal host to cut off the C2 channel.
Analyze proxy/firewall logs to identify the destination IP, domain, and URL.
Identify the process on the host generating the traffic.
Perform a reverse lookup and threat intelligence check on the C2 infrastructure.
Determine if any data exfiltration occurred over the C2 channel.
investigation_queries: index=proxy | stats count, stdev(duration) as time_variance by src_ip, dest_domain | where count > 1000 and time_variance < 5
DeviceNetworkEvents | where ActionType == 'ConnectionSuccess' | summarize count() by RemoteUrl, InitiatingProcessFileName | order by count_ desc
false_positive_checks: Verify if the traffic is legitimate software updates (e.g., WSUS, antivirus definition updates) or NTP synchronization.
Check if a legitimate web application utilizes long-polling or repetitive keep-alive API calls.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: C2 beaconing was detected from host {{hostname}} communicating with {{c2_domain_or_ip}}. Traffic analysis indicated a {{protocol}} channel established by process {{process_name}}. The host was isolated and the C2 IOCs added to the firewall blocklist. Full forensic investigation initiated.

playbooks.description: A malicious script (PHP, ASPX, JSP) uploaded to a web server to maintain persistent access and execute arbitrary commands.
indicators: Creation of new .php, .aspx, or .jsp files in web-accessible directories
HTTP requests to specific scripts that result in commands being executed by the web server process (e.g., w3wp.exe, apache2, nginx)
Unusual POST requests to typically static directories (e.g., /images/)
detection_patterns: File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) alert for new script file in web root
EDR alert: Web server process spawning cmd.exe, bash, or whoami
WAF alert for common web shell commands in HTTP parameters
response_steps: Acquire a copy of the suspected web shell file for analysis.
Review web access logs to identify the source IP that uploaded the shell and interacted with it.
Review EDR/process logs to see what commands the attacker executed via the shell.
Determine the initial vulnerability exploited to upload the shell (e.g., unrestricted file upload, RCE vulnerability).
investigation_queries: index=edr (ParentImage='*w3wp.exe' OR ParentImage='*httpd.exe' OR ParentImage='*nginx*') Image='*cmd.exe' OR Image='*sh'
index=web sourcetype=access_combined method=POST uri='*.php' status=200 | stats count by client_ip, uri
false_positive_checks: Verify with web developers if the file is a legitimate administrative script or API endpoint recently deployed.
Check if scheduled tasks or cron jobs legitimately trigger scripts that spawn shells.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: A web shell identified as {{filename}} was detected on internet-facing server {{hostname}}. The attacker accessed the shell from IP {{attacker_ip}} and executed commands including {{commands_executed}}. The server was isolated, the shell removed, and the underlying upload vulnerability is being patched.

playbooks.description: Insertion or injection of a SQL query via input data from the client to the application, attempting to manipulate the backend database.
indicators: HTTP GET/POST parameters containing SQL syntax (e.g., UNION SELECT, OR 1=1, WAITFOR DELAY)
Database errors returned in HTTP response bodies
Unusual database queries originating from the web application account that dump large tables
detection_patterns: WAF alert for SQLi signatures in URI or Payload
Database monitoring tool alerts on massive data extraction or schema querying from the app service account
response_steps: Validate if the attack was successful by checking HTTP response codes (e.g., 200 OK vs 500 Error) and response sizes.
Correlate WAF logs with backend Database logs to see if the malicious query actually executed.
Identify the vulnerable endpoint/parameter.
Check if sensitive data (PII, credentials) was exfiltrated.
investigation_queries: index=waf attack_type=SQLi | stats count by client_ip, uri, payload
index=database sourcetype=db_audit user=webapp_user query='*UNION SELECT*' OR query='*information_schema*'
false_positive_checks: Verify if a legitimate application feature passes complex queries or names with apostrophes (e.g., O'Connor) that trigger weak WAF rules.
Confirm if the activity is an authorized penetration test or DAST scanner.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: SQL Injection attack detected targeting the {{application_url}} endpoint. The attacker attempted to inject {{payload_snippet}}. Analysis of database logs indicates the query {{was_was_not}} successful. WAF rules were updated to block the attack and developers notified to sanitize inputs.

playbooks.description: A malicious attempt to disrupt the normal traffic of a targeted server, service, or network by overwhelming the target with a flood of Internet traffic.
indicators: Massive spike in inbound network traffic (bandwidth or packets-per-second)
Service degradation or complete unavailability of external-facing applications
High volume of SYN, UDP, or ICMP packets from highly distributed source IPs
detection_patterns: ISP or Anti-DDoS provider (Cloudflare, Akamai) alerts triggering for volumetric attack thresholds
Firewall CPU utilization spiking to 100% due to state table exhaustion
response_steps: Determine the type of DDoS attack (Volumetric, Protocol/State-exhaustion, or Application Layer/Layer 7).
Identify the targeted public IPs or URLs.
Engage the DDoS mitigation provider (e.g., Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS Shield) to verify automated mitigation is active or to request manual intervention.
Monitor backend infrastructure health to ensure the attack is not a smokescreen for intrusion.
investigation_queries: index=netflow | stats sum(bytes) as total_bytes by dest_ip | sort - total_bytes
index=waf uri='/login' | timechart span=1m count by client_ip
false_positive_checks: Check if the traffic spike is due to a legitimate marketing event, product launch, or viral social media post (Flash Crowd).
Verify if an internal script or misconfigured API client is causing an unintentional self-DDoS.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: A {{ddos_type}} DDoS attack targeted {{target_asset}} peaking at {{bandwidth_volume}}. Automated mitigation by {{ddos_provider}} successfully absorbed the traffic. Service experienced degradation for {{downtime_duration}} minutes before fully recovering. No secondary attacks detected during the event.

playbooks.description: A user logging into a service from two geographically distant locations within a timeframe that is physically impossible to travel between.
indicators: Successful logins for the same user from US and Asia within 1 hour
Authentication originating from a country where the organization does not operate or have employees
detection_patterns: SIEM/IdP alert calculating the distance and time between two consecutive logins and exceeding reasonable speed (e.g., > 1000 km/h)
response_steps: Determine which of the two logins represents the user's true location.
Check if the anomalous login utilized a VPN, Proxy, or Tor node.
Verify if MFA was satisfied for the anomalous login.
Review activity logs for the anomalous session to identify any malicious actions (e.g., inbox rule creation, file downloads).
investigation_queries: SigninLogs | where UserPrincipalName == 'user@domain.com' | project TimeGenerated, IPAddress, Location, ClientAppUsed, AppDisplayName | order by TimeGenerated desc
index=okta eventType=user.session.start | transaction user maxspan=4h | search eventcount>1 | table user, src_ip, geolocation
false_positive_checks: Verify if the user connected to a corporate or personal VPN that routes traffic out of a different country.
Check if the login was from a cloud-hosted application or server (e.g., a script running in AWS us-east-1) logging in on the user's behalf.
playbooks.incident_summary_template: Impossible travel was detected for user {{username}} with logins originating from {{location_1}} and {{location_2}} within {{timeframe}}. Investigation revealed the foreign login was {{malicious_or_benign_vpn}}. User sessions were revoked and password reset enforced.
